one_line_takeaway: A software-engineering (not security) empirical study mining 407 GitHub bug reports from 385 open-source Python MCP servers to build and survey-validate a five-category fault taxonomy (Server Setting, Server/Tool Configuration, Server/Host Configuration, Documentation, General Programming), finding tool-response-handling and documentation faults most commonly encountered by practitioners while tool-discovery/registration faults are rated most critical despite lower frequency.
key_finding: Server/Tool Configuration was the largest top-level fault category (133/419 labels, 31.74%), followed by Server/Host Configuration (120, 28.64%) and Server Setting (115, 27.45%); Documentation (29, 6.92%) and General Programming (22, 5.25%) were smallest. Within Server/Tool Configuration, Tool Call/Execution alone accounted for 63 issues. In the 41-respondent validation survey, every taxonomy fault type was reported as encountered by at least some practitioners (supporting completeness); Tool Response Handling (66.67%) and Documentation (64.52%) were the most frequently encountered fault types, while Host Authorization (13.33%), Host Logging (13.79%), and Host's Hook Configuration (16.67%) were least frequent. Despite a comparatively low 27.27% encounter rate, Tool Discovery & Registration had the highest perceived-criticality ratio (31.58% of respondents rated it 'critical'). Tool Authentication was reported as the most effort-intensive fault type to remediate. Statistical characterization (RQ3, Kruskal-Wallis on 5 categories) found small but significant effects for number of comments (p=0.0114, eta^2=0.031), comments per collaborator (p=0.0273, eta^2=0.026), and time-to-fix (p=0.0008, eta^2=0.045), but not for number of collaborators (p=0.255) or fixer experience (p=0.999). Post-hoc Dunn's tests (medium effect sizes, A12~0.30) showed Documentation issues receive fewer comments and comments-per-collaborator than Server/Host Configuration issues, and General Programming issues resolve faster than both Server/Host and Server/Tool Configuration issues. Comparing 407 MCP-related vs. 339 sampled non-MCP faults (Mann-Whitney U, Table 7): MCP faults had significantly more comments (p=0.0002, A12=0.575, small) and comments/collaborator (p=0.0004, A12=0.572, small), but were fixed faster (p=0.0081, A12=0.445, negligible) and by less-experienced fixers (p<0.0001, A12=0.334, medium) than non-MCP faults; collaborator counts did not differ significantly (p=0.286).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is the first large-scale empirical taxonomy of real-world faults in Model Context Protocol (MCP) server software, built bottom-up from manual open-coding of 407 MCP-related GitHub closed issues (out of 3,282 refined bug-related issues mined from 385 open-source repositories that use the Python MCP SDK). The taxonomy has five top-level categories with a total of 419 labels assigned across the 407 issues: Server Setting (115 issues, 27.45%), Server/Tool Configuration (133, 31.74%), Server/Host Configuration (120, 28.64%), Documentation (29, 6.92%), and General Programming (22, 5.25%), each further decomposed into subcategories (e.g., Server Dependency, Tool Call/Execution, Host Connection Configuration). The authors validate the taxonomy with a 41-respondent survey of MCP practitioners and characterize statistical differences between MCP-related and non-MCP-related faults (RQ3) using Kruskal-Wallis, Dunn's post-hoc, and Mann-Whitney U tests with effect sizes. Note: the paper's own Conclusion states the 407 issues came from '443 MCP server repositories,' which is inconsistent with the Methodology section's figure of 385 repositories after manual filtering (Table 2) -- this is a discrepancy in the paper's own text, not an artifact of this review.
threat_model: Not applicable -- this is an empirical software-engineering / reliability study, not a security paper, and it defines no adversary, attacker capability, or attack surface. It does not analyze exploitation, malicious tool servers, prompt injection attacks, or supply-chain compromise. The closest security-adjacent content is descriptive: two taxonomy leaves recorded as functional-fault categories rather than as a threat model -- 'Tool Authorization' (11/419 issues, e.g., a tool returning HTTP 403 due to missing Bedrock reranking permissions) and 'Tool Authentication' (8/419 issues, e.g., an MCP server failing OAuth because required Reddit credentials were mistakenly documented as optional) -- plus a single-issue subcategory called 'Security and Prompt Sanitization' (1 of 419 labels) described only as 'unsafe or unsanitized handling of user inputs in prompts or tool configurations... that expose tools to injection or other security risks,' with no worked example, exploit, or attacker model given. These are reliability/authorization-configuration bugs reported by developers on GitHub, not a security analysis, so this review does not construct a threat model on the paper's behalf.
limitations: Authors' own stated threats to validity (Section 6): (1) Construct validity -- GitHub closed issues are an imperfect proxy for real faults (mitigated by filtering to bug-labeled issues only); LLM-assisted classification/summarization may misclassify or drop context (mitigated by selecting the best of 9 models against a 40-issue ground truth, and by using LLMs only as a preprocessing aid ahead of manual clustering/labeling); the chosen fault-characteristic metrics (time-to-fix, comments, collaborators, fixer experience) may not fully capture severity or complexity. (2) Internal validity -- clustering design choices (embedding model, dimensionality reduction, cluster count) could bias which issues are flagged MCP-related, risking false negatives among the 3,282 candidate bug issues that were not exhaustively hand-reviewed (mitigated by a conservative keyword('MCP')-based backstop); open-coding introduces rater subjectivity (mitigated by two independent raters, periodic reconciliation meetings, and third-author adjudication, though no inter-rater agreement statistic could be computed for this open-coding phase since labels were not predefined). (3) External validity -- scope is limited to open-source, Python-SDK-based MCP servers; the authors explicitly do not claim generalization to closed-source servers, non-Python SDKs, or other LLM tool-integration paradigms. (4) Conclusion validity -- non-normal metric distributions required non-parametric tests; multiple comparisons were Bonferroni-corrected; findings are explicitly framed as descriptive/comparative, not causal. (5) Reliability -- non-deterministic components (LLMs, embeddings, stochastic clustering) could yield slightly different results on re-run; mitigated by reporting exact models/versions/prompts and releasing a replication package. Independently noted by this review (not the authors): the Conclusion states 407 issues were drawn from 443 repositories, which conflicts with the 385-repository figure given in the Methodology (Section 3.1, Table 2); this internal inconsistency was not flagged or resolved in the paper.

datasets_used: 13,555 candidate repositories identified via ~7,000 paginated GitHub Search API queries for Python-MCP-SDK usage
470 repositories after popularity/activity filtering (>=10 stars, >=10 forks, closed issues after Nov 1, 2024)
385 repositories after manual verification of genuine MCP functionality
30,795 closed issues extracted; 26,821 after removing non-English issues
3,591 bug-related closed issues (GPT-4o-mini classification); 3,282 after excluding 309 stale/duplicate/non-reproducible issues
407 MCP-related closed issues (383 from 9/101 BERTopic/KMeans clusters + keyword('MCP') matches) manually open-coded into 419 taxonomy labels
40-issue hand-labeled ground truth for evaluating issue-classification LLMs
41 valid survey responses from MCP practitioners (recruited from 10,756 collected contributor emails plus Discord communities)
339 randomly sampled non-MCP faults (95% confidence, 5% margin) used as a comparison group for RQ3
quantitative_results: Taxonomy category sizes (419 total labels): Server Setting 115 (27.45%), Server/Tool Configuration 133 (31.74%), Server/Host Configuration 120 (28.64%), Documentation 29 (6.92%), General Programming 22 (5.25%)

Issue-classification model comparison on 40 ground-truth issues (Table 1): GPT-4o-mini best (AC=0.77, PR=0.78, RC=0.82, F1=0.77); Llama3.1:8b close second (AC=0.77, PR=0.81, RC=0.82, F1=0.76); GPT-o4-mini worst (AC=0.64, F1=0.44)
Summarization-model comparison on 15 issues: GPT-4.1-mini best in 10/15 cases vs. GPT-4.1 in 3/15

Clustering: 101 KMeans clusters (via BERTopic, gemini-embedding-001 embeddings reduced to 5-D via UMAP); 9/101 clusters (383 issues) judged MCP-related with Fleiss' kappa 1.00 (calibration) / 0.94 (overall)
Survey fault-type encounter rates (Table 4): Tool Response Handling 66.67% and Documentation 64.52% highest; Host Authorization 13.33%, Host Logging 13.79%, Host's Hook Configuration 16.67% lowest
Tool Discovery & Registration: 27.27% encounter rate but 31.58% rated 'critical' -- highest criticality ratio of any fault type

RQ3 Kruskal-Wallis across 5 top-level categories (Table 5): comments p=0.0114 (eta^2=0.031, small), comments/collaborator p=0.0273 (eta^2=0.026, small), time-to-fix p=0.0008 (eta^2=0.045, small), collaborators p=0.2549 (n.s.), fixer experience p=0.9987 (n.s.)

Dunn's post-hoc (Table 6): Documentation vs. Server/Host Config differ in comments (p=0.0136, A12=0.0306 reported as medium) and comments/collaborator (p=0.0137, A12=0.2989, medium); General Programming vs. Server/Host Config and vs. Server/Tool Config differ in time-to-fix (p=0.0281 and p=0.0164 respectively, both medium effect)

MCP vs. non-MCP faults, Mann-Whitney U (Table 7, n=407 MCP vs. n=339 non-MCP): comments p=0.0002 (A12=0.5746, small), comments/collaborator p=0.0004 (A12=0.5719, small), time-to-fix p=0.0081 (A12=0.4454, negligible), fixer experience p<0.0001 (A12=0.3343, medium), collaborators p=0.2860 (n.s.)
baselines_compared: Issue-classification LLMs: Llama3.1:8b, Gemma3:4.3b, zephyr:7b, Mistral:7b, Qwen3:8b, GPT-4.1, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-o4-mini, GPT-4.1-mini (GPT-4o-mini selected as production classifier)
Summarization LLMs: GPT-4.1, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-o4-mini, GPT-4.1-mini, Llama3.1:8b (GPT-4.1-mini selected)

Statistical baselines: Kruskal-Wallis test (5-category comparison) vs. Dunn's post-hoc pairwise test vs. Mann-Whitney U test (MCP vs. non-MCP), each paired with an appropriate effect-size measure (eta-squared or Vargha-Delaney A12)
